name: MITRE.LIN.T1070.003.RULE Indicator Removal: Clear Command History
notes:
The following auditd rules should be enabled:

-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S rmdir -S unlink -S unlinkat -S rename -S renameat -F auid=0 -F path=/root/.bash_history -k siem-clear-bash
-w /root/.bash_history -p w -k siem-clear-bash

Get more Linux MITRE rules: https://www.scnsoft.com/services/security/siem/linux-mitre-attack-rules
conditions_text: when the event(s) were detected by one or more of Linux OS
when the event matches Linux Auditd Key (custom) is any of siem-clear-bash
when the event matches Command (custom) is not any of bash
when any of Linux UID (custom) are contained in any of MITRE: Linux Users - AlphaNumeric (Ignore Case)
TD_list: APPLY MITRE.LIN.T1070.003.RULE Indicator Removal: Clear Command History on events which are detected by the LOCAL system
AND when the event(s) were detected by one or more of Linux OS
AND when the event matches Linux Auditd Key (custom) is any of siem-clear-bash
AND when the event matches Command (custom) is not any of bash
AND NOT when any of Linux UID (custom) are contained in any of MITRE: Linux Users - AlphaNumeric (Ignore Case)
RED_list: Event name: MITRE.LIN.T1070.003.RULE Indicator Removal: Clear Command History

Event description: When a user logs off a system, this information is flushed to a file in the user's home directory called ~/.bash_history. Adversaries can abuse this by searching these files for cleartext passwords.
See more on: https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1070/003
Force the dispatched event to create a NEW offense, select the offense using Source IP
Include detected events by "Source IP" from this point forward, in the offense, for 0 second(s)
This information should contribute to the name of the associated offense(s)
